(2) Die Befugnis erstreckt sich jedoch nicht auf eine Verbreitung und Schaustellung, durch die ein
         berechtigtes Interesse des Abgebildeten oder, falls dieser verstorben ist, seiner Angehörigen
         verletzt wird.
For detailed discussion on the current state of German law, see Gerhard Stricker, ed., Urheberrecht.
Kommentar (Munich: Beck, 1999), commentary to §§ 22 and 23 (Ekkehard Gerstenberg and Horst-Peter
Götting), 926-965.
44
   Detailed discussion in Eberle, Dignity and Liberty, 87-92. For the general outlines of the German right,
see Otto Mühl and Walther Hadding, eds., Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (Stuttgart: Kohlhammer, 1998), § 823,
3 (Zeuner).

11
 image, rights to guarantee that people see you the way you want to be seen. They are, as

it were, rights to be shielded against unwanted public exposure—to be spared

embarrassment or humiliation. The prime enemy of our “privacy,” according to this

continental conception, is the media, which always threatens to broadcast unsavory

information about us in ways that endanger our public dignity. But of course any agent

that gathers and disseminates information can also pose dangers. In its focus on shielding

us from public indignity, the continental conception is typical of the continental legal

world much more broadly: On the Continent, the protection of personal dignity has been

a consuming concern for many generations.

America, by contrast, in this as in so many things, is much more oriented toward

values of liberty, and especially liberty over against the state. At its conceptual core, the

American right to privacy still takes much the form that it took in the eighteenth century:

It is the right to freedom from intrusions by the state, especially in one’s own home. 45

The prime danger, from the American point of view, is that “the sanctity of [our] homes,”

in the words of a leading nineteenth-century Supreme Court opinion, will be breached by

government actors. 46 American anxieties thus turn comparatively little on the media.

Instead, they tend to be anxieties about maintaining a kind of private sovereignty within

our own walls.

Such is the contrast that lies at the base of our diverge nt sensibilities about what

counts as a “privacy” violation. On the one hand, we have an Old World in which it

45
   For the commonplace that this is the origin of the American right to privacy: e.g. Rosen, Unwanted
Gaze, 5.
46
   Boyd v. United States, 116 U.S. 616, 630 (1886).

12
 seems fundamentally important not to lose public face 47 ; on the other a New World in

which it seems fundamentally important to preserve the home as a citadel of individual

sovereignty. When Americans seem to continental Europeans to violate norms of

privacy, it is because they seem to display an embarrassing lack of concern for public

dignity—whether the issue is the public indignity inflicted upon Monica Lewinsky by the

media, or the self- inflicted indignity of an American who boasts about his salary.
